content.problemStatement:
Chef has a string SSS with him. Chef is happy if the string contains a **contiguous substring** of length **strictly greater** than 222 in which all its characters are vowels.

Determine whether Chef is happy or not.

Note that, in english alphabet, vowels are `a`, `e`, `i`, `o`, and `u`.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains of a single line of input, a string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, if Chef is happy, print `HAPPY` else print `SAD`.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `hAppY`, `Happy`, `haPpY`, and `HAPPY` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: S contains only lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T lines follow, each containing a single string S consisting of only lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "HAPPY" if the string contains a contiguous substring of length strictly greater than 2 consisting only of vowels (a, e, i, o, u), otherwise output "SAD".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: "aeiou" is all vowels and length 5 > 2, so HAPPY.
Test case 2: No vowel-only substring longer than 2, so SAD.
Test case 3: Same reason, SAD.
Test case 4: Substring "eea" has length 3 > 2 and consists only of vowels, so HAPPY.
"aei" is a contiguous vowel substring of length 3 (>2), therefore Chef is happy.
First string has no vowel run longer than 2, so SAD. Second string contains "aaa" (length 3) which makes Chef HAPPY.

solution.approach: For each string we scan from left to right keeping a counter of the current length of a consecutive vowel segment. When the current character is a vowel we increment the counter, otherwise we reset it to zero. If at any point the counter becomes greater than 2 we can immediately conclude the answer is HAPPY for that test case. If the scan finishes without the counter exceeding 2, the answer is SAD. This runs in O(|S|) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|S|) per test case, overall O(T * max|S|).
